preamble: The shift from the pharyngeal fricative [ʕ] (the Semitic 'Ayin') to the near-open front vowel [æ] is a common form of vocalization or substitution in loanword adaptation. When borrowed into languages lacking pharyngeal consonants (like English), the acoustic resonance of the Ayin is often interpreted as a low front vowel.

examples.note: The pharyngeal consonant is dropped, and its acoustic effect on the adjacent vowel is adapted as the English 'ash' vowel.
The initial Ayin is vocalized/substituted with the front vowel in Anglicized pronunciation.
